findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a vertebral body wedge fracture accompanied by complete loss of normal T1 fatty marrow signal and replacement by a soft tissue mass. The posterior border of the vertebral body is convex and expanded, extending into the spinal canal.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include involvement of the posterior elements, such as the pedicles, and the presence of an associated irregular epidural or paraspinal soft tissue mass. Additionally, there is restricted diffusion on MRI within the replaced marrow.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of a fluid cleft sign or preserved normal marrow signal to suggest a benign osteoporotic collapse. Furthermore, there is no disk space narrowing or endplate erosion to suggest an infectious spondylodiscitis.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the convex bulging of the posterior vertebral border combined with an associated epidural soft-tissue mass and pedicle involvement.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Pathologic fracture (metastatic disease), given the complete geographic replacement of normal marrow signal, the expanded posterior contour, and the presence of an epidural soft tissue mass. Metastatic disease frequently targets the posterior elements and vertebral body before leading to pathological collapse.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Traumatic fracture, however traumatic fractures typically preserve some normal marrow signal and present with a smooth, rim-shaped paraspinal hematoma rather than an irregular nodular encasing mass. Furthermore, traumatic fractures usually demonstrate sharp fracture lines with retropulsed bone fragments rather than extensive geographic marrow replacement.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Insu̧  ciency fracture, given it is a very common cause of vertebral wedging in the elderly, and misdiagnosing a benign osteoporotic collapse as metastatic could lead to unnecessary oncological interventions and severe psychological distress. However, insufficiency fractures typically spare the pedicles, often exhibit a fluid cleft sign, and demonstrate signal loss on opposed-phase imaging, which is absent here.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to assess the spinal canal for evidence of malignant cord compression and evaluate the stability of the spine.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is vital to look for previously documented benign osteoporotic fractures or features to support a known systemic malignant disease, helping establish chronicity and progression.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a whole-spine MRI, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the extent of epidural disease, confirming the presence of occult multifocal marrow metastases, and precisely grading any spinal cord compression.
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1-weighted sequences to assess for geographic marrow replacement, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) as malignant fractures typically show restricted diffusion, and in- and out-of-phase imaging because malignant lesions typically show no loss of signal on opposed-phase sequences.
management.nuclearMedicine: A PET-CT or nuclear medicine bone scan would be beneficial to screen the entire skeleton for further metastatic deposits and locate an occult primary malignancy if not already known.
management.intervention: If a primary tumor is unknown and the patient is neurologically stable, I would specify an image-guided transpedicular bone biopsy targeting the solid paraspinal or vertebral soft-tissue mass to obtain a definitive histological diagnosis.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Spinal Surgery and Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the degree of vertebral body collapse, the exact extent of any epidural soft tissue component, and any impending or actual spinal cord compression to guide urgent surgical decompression or palliative radiotherapy.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a vertebral body wedge fracture accompanied by complete loss of normal T1 fatty marrow signal and replacement by a soft tissue mass. The posterior border of the vertebral body is convex and expanded, extending into the spinal canal. Supporting features include involvement of the posterior elements, such as the pedicles, and the presence of an associated irregular epidural or paraspinal soft tissue mass. Additionally, there is restricted diffusion on MRI within the replaced marrow. Importantly, there is no evidence of a fluid cleft sign or preserved normal marrow signal to suggest a benign osteoporotic collapse. Furthermore, there is no disk space narrowing or endplate erosion to suggest an infectious spondylodiscitis. The feature that most influences my thinking is the convex bulging of the posterior vertebral border combined with an associated epidural soft-tissue mass and pedicle involvement.

The appearances are most in keeping with Pathologic fracture (metastatic disease), given the complete geographic replacement of normal marrow signal, the expanded posterior contour, and the presence of an epidural soft tissue mass. Metastatic disease frequently targets the posterior elements and vertebral body before leading to pathological collapse. The principle differential would be Traumatic fracture, however traumatic fractures typically preserve some normal marrow signal and present with a smooth, rim-shaped paraspinal hematoma rather than an irregular nodular encasing mass. Furthermore, traumatic fractures usually demonstrate sharp fracture lines with retropulsed bone fragments rather than extensive geographic marrow replacement. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Insu̧  ciency fracture, given it is a very common cause of vertebral wedging in the elderly, and misdiagnosing a benign osteoporotic collapse as metastatic could lead to unnecessary oncological interventions and severe psychological distress. However, insufficiency fractures typically spare the pedicles, often exhibit a fluid cleft sign, and demonstrate signal loss on opposed-phase imaging, which is absent here.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to assess the spinal canal for evidence of malignant cord compression and evaluate the stability of the spine. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is vital to look for previously documented benign osteoporotic fractures or features to support a known systemic malignant disease, helping establish chronicity and progression. I would recommend a whole-spine MRI, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely evaluating the extent of epidural disease, confirming the presence of occult multifocal marrow metastases, and precisely grading any spinal cord compression. I would specify T1-weighted sequences to assess for geographic marrow replacement, diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) as malignant fractures typically show restricted diffusion, and in- and out-of-phase imaging because malignant lesions typically show no loss of signal on opposed-phase sequences. A PET-CT or nuclear medicine bone scan would be beneficial to screen the entire skeleton for further metastatic deposits and locate an occult primary malignancy if not already known. If a primary tumor is unknown and the patient is neurologically stable, I would specify an image-guided transpedicular bone biopsy targeting the solid paraspinal or vertebral soft-tissue mass to obtain a definitive histological diagnosis. This should go to the Spinal Surgery and Oncology MDT. My report would clearly document the degree of vertebral body collapse, the exact extent of any epidural soft tissue component, and any impending or actual spinal cord compression to guide urgent surgical decompression or palliative radiotherapy.
